This program has a logic error, so we have to debug it.

 RULE: Every function needs to be called in order to execute. This includes the main function.

Click  Run and you will see that nothing happens on the stage.
Fix the program so that the stage will be set. Note: Make sure every function is called!
Click  Run to test if you fixed the program. When it is fixed, click  Submit and  Next.


#This program will recreate the story of the 3 little pigs

def main():

# Call the main function to start the program
main()

```python
#This program will recreate the story of the 3 little pigs

def set_stage():
    """ Sets up the stage for the start of the story """
    stage.set_background("barn")
    sprite = codesters.Sprite("pig",-100,-125)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    set_stage()

    # Add any additional code here to call other functions or complete the story

# Call the main function to start the program
main()
``` 

Now, when you run this code, the stage should be set up with a background and a pig sprite. Feel free to complete the story by adding more code after the `set_stage()` function call.

def add_sprite():
    """ Add cow sprite to the stage and set its speed """
    
    cow = codesters.Sprite("cow",-400, -150)
    cow.set_x_speed(3)
    return cow
    
def setup_stage():
    """ Set up the stage with background and sprites """
    
    stage.set_background("barn")
    stage.disable_right_wall()
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",-150,-150)
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",0,-150)
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",150,-150)
    
def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
    
    if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
        sprite.say("yum!",.2)
        hit_sprite.hide()
    
def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    sprite = add_sprite()

main()



This program has a logic error. The cow is hungry and wants to eat the hay, but nothing happens!

 RULE: An event handler links a sprite to a function that is called whenever the event is triggered

The issue here is that although you have defined a function `collision(sprite, hit_sprite)`, you have not linked it as an event handler for any specific event. In this case, you want `collision` function to be called when the cow sprite collides with the hay sprite.

To fix the logic error and make the cow eat hay when it collides with it, you need to use the `set_collision_handler` method on the cow sprite to link the `collision` function to the 'collision' event. Here is the corrected code:

```python
def add_sprite():
    """ Add cow sprite to the stage and set its speed """
    
    cow = codesters.Sprite("cow",-400, -150)
    cow.set_x_speed(3)
    return cow

def setup_stage():
    """ Set up the stage with background and sprites """
    
    stage.set_background("barn")
    stage.disable_right_wall()
    hay1 = codesters.Sprite("hay",-150,-150)
    hay2 = codesters.Sprite("hay",0,-150)
    hay3 = codesters.Sprite("hay",150,-150)

def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
    
    if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
        sprite.say("yum!", .2)
        hit_sprite.hide()

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    sprite = add_sprite()
    sprite.set_collision_handler(collision)

main()
```

Now, when the cow sprite collides with the hay sprite, the `collision` function will be called, and the cow will say "yum!" and hide the hay sprite.

set_stage()



This program has a bug, or error. So let's debug it!

 RULE: Docstrings are a kind of comment that start and end with 3 sets of quotes around the text.

Click  Run and read the error message that pops up. Then click OK.
Fix the docstring so that it starts and ends with 3 quotes. It will turn green when it's correct!
Click  Run to see if you fixed the program. When it is fixed, click  Submit and  Next.

Here is the corrected code with the fixed docstring:

```python
def set_stage():
    """Sets the background and adds a sprite to the stage"""
    stage.set_background("jungle")
    sprite = codesters.Sprite("toucan")

set_stage()
```

The issue was with the docstring not having the correct format of starting and ending with 3 quotes. It has been fixed in the code above. When you run this corrected code, it should set the background to "jungle" and add a toucan sprite to the stage.

def add_sprite():
    """ Add cow sprite to the stage and set its speed """
    
    cow = codesters.Sprite("cow",-400, -150)
    cow.set_x_speed(3)
    return cow
    
def setup_stage():
    """ Set up the stage with background and sprites """
    
    stage.set_background("barn")
    stage.disable_right_wall()
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",-150,-150)
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",0,-150)
    hay = codesters.Sprite("hay",150,-150)
    
def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
    
    if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
        sprite.say("yum!",.2)
        hit_sprite.hide()
    
def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    sprite = add_sprite()

main()



This program has a logic error. The cow is hungry and wants to eat the hay, but nothing happens!

 RULE: An event handler links a sprite to a function that is called whenever the event is triggered.
Click  Run and see what happens. Look in the code to see where the cow should be eating the hay.
Fix the program by adding an event handler to the main() function so that the collision event happens.
Click  Run to test if you fixed the program. When it is fixed, click  Submit and  Next.

To fix the logic error in the program and make the cow eat the hay when they collide, you need to link the `collision` function as an event handler for the cow sprite when it collides with the hay sprite.

Here is the corrected code with the addition of the event handler in the `main()` function:

```python
def add_sprite():
    """ Add cow sprite to the stage and set its speed """
    
    cow = codesters.Sprite("cow",-400, -150)
    cow.set_x_speed(3)
    return cow

def setup_stage():
    """ Set up the stage with background and sprites """
    
    stage.set_background("barn")
    stage.disable_right_wall()
    hay1 = codesters.Sprite("hay",-150,-150)
    hay2 = codesters.Sprite("hay",0,-150)
    hay3 = codesters.Sprite("hay",150,-150)

def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
    
    if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
        sprite.say("yum!", .2)
        hit_sprite.hide()

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    sprite = add_sprite()
    sprite.set_gravity_off()
    sprite.set_collision_handler(collision)

main()
```

By adding `sprite.set_collision_handler(collision)` in the `main()` function, you are setting up the event handler so that when the cow sprite collides with the hay sprite, the `collision` function will be triggered, making the cow say "yum!" and hiding the hay sprite. When you run this corrected code, it should work as expected.
Fix the program by adding an event handler to the main() function so that the collision event happens.

To add an event handler in the `main()` function so that the collision event happens when the cow sprite collides with the hay sprite, you need to use the `set_collision_handler` method on the cow sprite to link the `collision` function to the collision event.

Here is the updated `main()` function with the event handler added:

```python
def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    cow = add_sprite()
    
    def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
        """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
        
        if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
            sprite.say("yum!", .2)
            hit_sprite.hide()
    
    cow.set_collision_handler(collision)

main()
```

By adding the `collision` function inside the `main()` function and setting it as the collision handler for the cow sprite using `cow.set_collision_handler(collision)`, you ensure that the `collision` function will be called when the cow collides with the hay. This approach will now allow the cow to eat the hay when they collide.
name 'setup_stage' is not defined on line: 4

It seems that the `setup_stage` function is being called before it is defined in the code. To resolve this issue, you can rearrange the order of function definitions, ensuring that the `setup_stage` function is defined before it is called in the `main()` function.

Here is the corrected code with the function definitions reordered:

```python
def add_sprite():
    """ Add cow sprite to the stage and set its speed """
    
    cow = codesters.Sprite("cow",-400, -150)
    cow.set_x_speed(3)
    return cow

def setup_stage():
    """ Set up the stage with background and sprites """
    
    stage.set_background("barn")
    stage.disable_right_wall()
    hay1 = codesters.Sprite("hay",-150,-150)
    hay2 = codesters.Sprite("hay",0,-150)
    hay3 = codesters.Sprite("hay",150,-150)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    setup_stage()
    cow = add_sprite()
    
    def collision(sprite, hit_sprite):
        """ Collision event for the cow to eat hay """
        
        if hit_sprite.get_image_name() == "hay":
            sprite.say("yum!", .2)
            hit_sprite.hide()
    
    cow.set_collision_handler(collision)

main()
```

By placing the `setup_stage` function above the `main()` function, the code should now run without any errors related to the function not being defined. When you run this corrected code, the collision event should work as intended when the cow collides with the hay sprite.

def set_stage():
    """ Sets up the stage for the game """
    
    stage.set_background("soccerfield")
    stage.disable_floor()

def add_player():
    """ Adds a player to the stage for the user to control """

    player = codesters.Sprite("player1")
    player.go_to(0, -155)
    return player

def add_ball():
    """ Adds a ball to the stage and sets its attributes """
    
    ball = codesters.Sprite("soccerball")
    ball.set_y_speed(-8)

def head_ball(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Detects collisions between the player and ball """
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_y_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_y_speed(-my_var + 1)
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_x_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_x_speed(my_var + 1)
    
def move_left():
    """ Moves the player left """
    
    player.move_left(50)

def move_right():
    """ Moves the player right """
    
    player.move_right(50)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    set_stage()
    player = add_player()
    add_ball()
    player.event_collision(head_ball)
    
    stage.event_key("left", move_left)
    stage.event_key("right", move_right)

main()



Final step! Let's link the move_right() function to the right arrow key.

Go to  and drag Sprite Key Event Handler into the bottom of the main function.

use this 
sprite.event_key("right", right_key)

At the start of the line, change sprite to player.
The second parameter is the function called when the key is pressed. Replace right_key with move_righ

To link the `move_right()` function to the right arrow key in the game, we need to set up a key event handler for the player sprite to respond to the right arrow key. Here is the updated `main()` function with the key event handler added:

```python
def set_stage():
    """ Sets up the stage for the game """
    
    stage.set_background("soccerfield")
    stage.disable_floor()

def add_player():
    """ Adds a player to the stage for the user to control """

    player = codesters.Sprite("player1")
    player.go_to(0, -155)
    return player

def add_ball():
    """ Adds a ball to the stage and sets its attributes """
    
    ball = codesters.Sprite("soccerball")
    ball.set_y_speed(-8)

def head_ball(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Detects collisions between the player and ball """
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_y_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_y_speed(-my_var + 1)
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_x_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_x_speed(my_var + 1)
    
def move_left():
    """ Moves the player left """
    
    player.move_left(50)

def move_right():
    """ Moves the player right """
    
    player.move_right(50)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    set_stage()
    player = add_player()
    add_ball()
    player.event_collision(head_ball)
    
    stage.event_key("left", move_left)
    
    player.event_key("right", move_right)

main()
```

In the updated `main()` function, the event handler has been added for the right arrow key to make the player sprite move right when the right arrow key is pressed. This should allow you to control the player sprite using the left and right arrow keys in the game.

def set_stage():
    """ Sets up the stage for the game """
    
    stage.set_background("soccerfield")
    stage.disable_floor()

def add_player():
    """ Adds a player to the stage for the user to control """

    player = codesters.Sprite("player1")
    player.go_to(0, -155)
    return player

def add_ball():
    """ Adds a ball to the stage and sets its attributes """
    
    ball = codesters.Sprite("soccerball")
    ball.set_y_speed(-8)

def head_ball(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Detects collisions between the player and ball """
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_y_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_y_speed(-my_var + 1)
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_x_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_x_speed(my_var + 1)
    
def move_left():
    """ Moves the player left """
    
    player.move_left(50)

def move_right():
    """ Moves the player right """
    
    player.move_right(50)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    set_stage()
    player = add_player()
    add_ball()
    player.event_collision(head_ball)
    
    stage.event_key("left", move_left)
    
    player.event_key("right", move_right)

main()



Let's make this game more challenging by adding another ball and giving each ball a different speed!

Add another ball! Place another Function Call in your main function which calls add_ball().
Set the ball speed! Add the parameter speed to your add_ball() function definition.
Replace -8 in set_y_speed() with the new parameter speed.
In main(), add integer parameters to the add_ball() function calls. Make sure they are negative!

and     my_function()

To add another ball to the game and give each ball a different speed, you need to make some modifications to the existing code based on the instructions provided. Here is the modified code with the changes made:

```python
def set_stage():
    """ Sets up the stage for the game """
    
    stage.set_background("soccerfield")
    stage.disable_floor()

def add_player():
    """ Adds a player to the stage for the user to control """

    player = codesters.Sprite("player1")
    player.go_to(0, -155)
    return player

def add_ball(speed):
    """ Adds a ball to the stage and sets its attributes """
    
    ball = codesters.Sprite("soccerball")
    ball.set_y_speed(speed)

def head_ball(sprite, hit_sprite):
    """ Detects collisions between the player and ball """
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_y_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_y_speed(-my_var + 1)
    
    my_var = hit_sprite.get_x_speed()
    hit_sprite.set_x_speed(my_var + 1)
    
def move_left():
    """ Moves the player left """
    
    player.move_left(50)

def move_right():
    """ Moves the player right """
    
    player.move_right(50)

def main():
    """ Sets up the program and calls other functions """
    
    set_stage()
    player = add_player()
    
    add_ball(-8)  # Adding the first ball with speed -8
    add_ball(-6)  # Adding the second ball with speed -6
    
    player.event_collision(head_ball)
    
    stage.event_key("left", move_left)
    
    player.event_key("right", move_right)

main()
```

In the modified code:
- The `add_ball` function now takes a parameter `speed` to set the speed of the added ball.
- Two calls to `add_ball` have been added in the `main()` function with different speed values for each ball (-8 and -6).
- By passing different speed values, each ball will have a different speed setting, making the game more challenging. 

These modifications should now add two balls with different speeds to the game.
